commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=72541face9bed27704d991b63596710c335e7221)
commands.review_summary:
This PR updates Telegram group docs and adds a non-mutating `openclaw doctor` warning with tests for multi-account Telegram configs that keep group rules only at the root channel scope.

PR surface: Source +69, Tests +155, Docs +38. Total +262 across 5 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows the PR scanner ignores `channels.defaults.groupPolicy` and Telegram's runtime fallback, while current runtime uses both when deciding group authorization.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] A narrow automated repair can align the new warning with runtime policy and add missing tests without changing Telegram runtime authorization or the docs direction.

target.title: docs(telegram): clarify account-local group config
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram docs pointed group setup at root \`channels.telegram.groups\`, which is easy to misread in multi-account configs.
- Why it matters: a bot account can receive a group message from Telegram but still drop it during OpenClaw group policy/auth because the active account has no account-local \`groups\` rule.
- What changed: documented account-local Telegram group rules for multi-account setups and added a non-mutating doctor warning for root groups plus enabled allowlisted accounts missing \`accounts.<id>.groups\`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): runtime authorization and group inheritance behavior are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: A multi-account Telegram setup had a root \`channels.telegram.groups\` rule for a group, but the active bot account lacked \`channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups\`. A bot-authored group mention was delivered to the gateway but dropped before agent dispatch.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw gateway with multiple Telegram bot accounts and a private Telegram group used for bot-to-bot coordination. Identifiers are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Verified the local working configuration by copying the group rule into \`channels.telegram.accounts.default.groups["<GROUP_CHAT_ID>"]\`, restarting the installed gateway, and sending a bot-authored \`REQUEST @<bot> ...\` group message from a second trusted bot account.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied redacted runtime log output from the local gateway after the account-local group rule was added:

\`\`\`text
Inbound message telegram:group:<GROUP_CHAT_ID> -> @<BOT_USERNAME> (group, 168 chars)
⇄ res ✓ message.action 310ms channel=telegram conn=<REDACTED> id=<REDACTED>
\`\`\`

- Observed result after fix: Account-local Telegram group config fixed the real setup without a runtime code change. This PR documents that path and adds a doctor warning so similar configs are easier to diagnose.
- What was not tested: I did not deploy this branch as a packaged OpenClaw release. The runtime behavior is unchanged; the new doctor warning is covered by focused tests.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Copied redacted runtime log output from the failing trial before the account-local group rule existed:

\`\`\`text
[telegram][diag] spooled update <UPDATE_ID> completed without agent dispatch kind=message chat=<GROUP_CHAT_ID> chatType=group message=<MESSAGE_ID> from=<TRUSTED_BOT_USER_ID> fromBot=true fromUsername=<TRUSTED_BOT_USERNAME> text="REQUEST @<BOT_USERNAME> <test-message>" outcome=dropped reason=group-policy-or-auth
\`\`\`

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The docs emphasized root \`channels.telegram.groups\` for group setup, while multi-account users need to reason about the active account's group rules.
- Missing detection / guardrail: \`openclaw doctor\` did not flag a config where root Telegram groups existed but enabled allowlisted accounts had no account-local group rules.
- Contributing context (if known): Existing troubleshooting guidance made Telegram privacy/raw delivery and group policy/auth look similar until a diagnostic identified \`group-policy-or-auth\`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: \`extensions/telegram/src/doctor.test.ts\`
- Scenario the test should lock in: root \`channels.telegram.groups\` exists in a multi-account Telegram config, and an enabled allowlisted account has no \`channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups\`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the changed behavior is a non-mutating doctor warning, so the focused doctor scanner/formatter tests cover the new surface directly.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing Telegram doctor tests covered invalid sender IDs, apiRoot warnings, and first-time group guidance, but not this multi-account root-groups trap.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

\`openclaw doctor\` can now warn about multi-account Telegram configs where root group rules exist but enabled allowlisted accounts have no account-local group rules. Telegram docs now show the account-local group config path for multi-account setups.

## Diagram (if applicable)

\`\`\`text
Before:
multi-account Telegram config -> root channels.telegram.groups only -> group update can be dropped by account policy/auth

After:
multi-account Telegram config -> channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups -> account has explicit group authorization
\`\`\`

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any \`Yes\`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw source checkout for tests; installed OpenClaw gateway for real behavior proof
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram
- Relevant config (redacted):

\`\`\`json5
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      accounts: {
        default: {
          groups: {
            "<GROUP_CHAT_ID>": {
              requireMention: true,
              allowFrom: ["<OWNER_USER_ID>", "<TRUSTED_BOT_USER_ID>"],
            },
          },
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
\`\`\`

### Steps

1. Configure multiple Telegram bot accounts.
2. Put a group rule only under root \`channels.telegram.groups\`.
3. Send a trusted bot-authored group mention to the active bot account.
4. Move/copy the group rule to \`channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups\`.
5. Restart the gateway and resend the trusted bot-authored group mention.

### Expected

- Docs make the account-local group path clear for multi-account configs.
- Doctor warns when root groups exist but enabled allowlisted accounts are missing account-local group rules.
- A correctly configured active account receives the trusted group mention as a normal agent turn.

### Actual

- Before the local config fix, the group message was raw-delivered but dropped during group policy/auth.
- After account-local group config, the trusted bot-authored group mention dispatched normally.
- With this PR, focused tests confirm the new doctor warning and skip cases.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local real setup with multi-account Telegram bots; root group rule did not authorize the active account, account-local group rule did.
- Edge cases checked: disabled accounts, \`groupPolicy: "open"\` accounts, mixed open plus allowlisted accounts, single-account configs.
- What you did **not** verify: full packaged release build or live doctor command from an installed package containing this patch.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The doctor warning could be noisy for accounts intentionally configured without group allowlists.
  - Mitigation: The warning skips disabled accounts and accounts whose effective \`groupPolicy\` is \`open\` or \`disabled\`, and tests cover those cases.
target.author: hyjkimbot

commands.reason: PR is not opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge

commands.author: michmill1970

target.title: External reranker
target.body:
## Summary What problem does this PR solve? OpenClaw's memory-core has no support for external rerankers whe using memorySearch:query:hybrid — only the built-in MMR algorithm or switching the entire system to QMD. QMD is local-only, so the reranker runs on the gateway. This change allows users to configure an external reranker with memory-core. Why does this matter now? It increases the quality of results from memory searches. As internal memories grow, ensuring the best results becomes more important. What is the intended outcome? Better results from memory searches. What is intentionally out of scope? QMD, LanceDB, and other memory systems for Openclaw. What does success look like? Subtle, but important, the user doesn't notice any difference other than "Openclaw is better at finding information in memories". What should reviewers focus on? Look at the interface used between memory-core and MMR. MMR has been moved to a plugin, and the new external reranker uses the same interface.
Summary guidance This PR makes two changes. First, it moves mmr to a bundled plugin called memory-mmr. Second, it adds a memory-external-reranker plugin to memory-core, enabling external reranker support for Cohere-compatible /v1/rerank endpoints. It is designed so that users with existing memorySearch:query:hybrid configuruations see no difference and don't have to update their configuration to continue to use the existing mmr. Only when the user enables and memory-external-reranker plugin and adds the other necessary config entries will the external reranker be used. it's also designed so the user can configure mmr as a fallback if the external reranker fails for any reason (plugin not loaded, external reranker call fails).
## Linked context Which issue does this close? Closes #64438 Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? Related # Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
Linked context guidance https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64438
## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: external reranker called during memory recall - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Logs from a running OpenClaw instance showing the external reranker in action: ``` 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] reranking with provider=local-llm-server-02-8082 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 fallbacks=none topN=30 documents=34 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 url=http://local-llm-server-02:8082/v1/rerank topN=30 documents=34 ``` ... ``` 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 response status=200 ok=true 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 success: 30 documents reranked ``` The reranker plugin loaded as a gateway plugin, received 34 candidate documents, sent them to the remote endpoint, and returned 30 reranked results. - Observed result after fix: better search results from memory - What was not tested: QMD, LanceDB, other memory systems - Proof limitations or environment constraints: - Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
Real behavior proof guidance config json fragments agents:defaults: ``` "memorySearch": { "enabled": true, "provider": "openai-compatible", "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "query": { "hybrid": { "enabled": true, "vectorWeight": 0.7, "textWeight": 0.3, "mmr": { "enabled": true, "lambda": 0.7, "provider": "memory-external-reranker", "fallback": "memory-mmr" } } }, "remote": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8081/v1", "apiKey": { "source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "LOCAL_LLM_02_KEY" }, "batch": { "enabled": false }, "nonBatchConcurrency": 3 }, } ``` models:providers: ``` "local-llm-server-02-8082": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8082", "models": [ { "id": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "name": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "input": [ "text" ] } ] } ``` plugins:entries: ``` "memory-external-reranker": { "enabled": true, "config": { "provider": "local-llm-server-02-8082", "model": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "topN": 30 } }, ``` - Real environment tested: multiple test VMs. I've been running it on my production Openclaw instance for over a week. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enable and configure the memory-external-reranker plugin, index memory, perform a memory search like "What do you remember about the Lucid Air?" See attached screenshot.
## Tests and validation Which commands did you run? pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test pnpm test:extension memory-external-reranker pnpm test:extension memory-mmr What regression coverage was added or updated? Full tests added for both MMR plugin and new external reranker plugin. What failed before this fix, if known? If no test was added, why not?
Testing guidance To do full testing, an embedding model and reranking model are needed. I use llamacpp on localhost and update openclaw.json to point to the local models. ``` ./llama-server -hf Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-GGUF --port 8081 --embedding ./llama-server -hf ggml-org/Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B-Q8_0-GGUF --port 8082 --embedding --reranking --pooling rank ```
## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? No Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? Yes. New config entries added Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No. Uses existing patterns and code to call external reranker. What is the highest-risk area? Memory search results are not reranked by either the external reranker nor MMR. How is that risk mitigated? MMR is a bundled plugin. As long as mmr is a buldled plugin and loaded by default, the risk is minimal.
Risk guidance Minimal risk once MMR is a bundled plugin.
## Current review state What is the next action? What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
Review state guidance Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.
target.author: michmill1970
